title: NIS2 Art.28-30 domain registration, DNS integrity and information-sharing evidence
exclusions: Does not administer registrar portals, rotate DNSSEC keys, or attest contractual relationships with ISPs; cooperation evidence is technical telemetry only unless legal adopts the feed.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.55`, supporting `dns:query` / `infoblox:dns` slices, STIX feed statistics, registry lookup versions, and tamper-evident export in `index=audit_evidence`.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.28 receives partial assurance because UC evidence proves the monitorable workflow or telemetry portion while legal approval and filings remain outside Splunk.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Point a lab resolver at a test zone that returns five unexpected additional `answer` values for a production `fqdn` listed in `domain_registry.csv` with a low `max_answers_allowed`, then verify `ns_drift=1` appears in the panel within the 24-hour window.
controlTest.negativeScenario: When answers stay within the registry‚Äôs allowed cardinality and the STIX domain feed reports at least one fresh `value` per `feed_name` in the last week, the alert stays quiet across a replay test harness that excludes deliberate fault injection.

dataSources: `index=dns` `sourcetype=dns:query` or `sourcetype=infoblox:dns` with `query`, `answer`, `response_code`, `src_ip` from Splunk Add-on for Infoblox ([Splunkbase 2934](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2934)); CIM **Network_Resolution** data model fields `dest`, `src`, `answer`; `lookup=domain_registry.csv` for approved nameservers and registrar IDs; `index=threat_intel` `sourcetype=stix`; optional `index=grc` `sourcetype=grc:sharing_arrangement`.
app: Splunk Add-on for Infoblox ([Splunkbase 2934](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2934)); Splunk CIM ([Splunkbase 1621](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1621)); Splunk Enterprise Security ([Splunkbase 263](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/263)) for intel workflow.
spl:
| inputlookup domain_registry.csv
| rename fqdn as query
| join query type=left [
    search index=dns (sourcetype=dns:query OR sourcetype=infoblox:dns) earliest=-24h
    | stats dc(answer) as answer_cardinality values(answer) as last_answers by query
]
| eval max_ans=coalesce(max_answers_allowed,5)
| eval ns_drift=if(isnotnull(answer_cardinality) AND answer_cardinality>max_ans,1,0)
| append [
    search index=threat_intel sourcetype=stix earliest=-7d threat_type=domain
    | stats dc(value) as observed_domains by feed_name
    | where observed_domains<1
]
| lookup nis2_dns_kfp_exceptions query OUTPUT exception_until
| where ns_drift=1 OR (isnotnull(feed_name) AND observed_domains<1)
| where isnull(exception_until) OR now()>strptime(exception_until,"%Y-%m-%d")
| table query, registrar_tag, answer_cardinality, last_answers, feed_name, observed_domains, ns_drift
description: Correlates the approved domain registry lookup against live DNS answer diversity per fully qualified name and adds a lightweight STIX domain-feed health check so registrar data, resolution integrity, and cooperation telemetry stay aligned for digital infrastructure teams.
value: Brand-protection and public-trust leaders can show regulators that name-resolution anomalies and stale information-sharing feeds are operationally monitored alongside the legal register, narrowing exposure during coordinated takedowns.
implementation: (1) Deploy Splunkbase 2934 for Infoblox or map BIND logs to `dns:query`. (2) Populate `domain_registry.csv`. (3) Inhibit alert noise with `nis2_dns_kfp_exceptions`. (4) Integrate STIX feeds via ES. (5) Archive weekly PDF to `index=audit_evidence`.
visualization: DNS answer cardinality heatmap, table of `query` vs expected nameservers, intel feed single values.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Network_Resolution where nodename=Network_Resolution.DNS by Network_Resolution.query span=1h
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate the Network_Resolution data model (summary range 30 days) before relying on `tstats` variants during peak query volume.

references.title: Splunk Common Information Model ‚ÄĒ Network Resolution

knownFalsePositives:
‚ÄĘ **GeoDNS or load-balancing pools** ‚ÄĒ Distinguish: many legitimate `answer` values during rotation. Suppress: maintain `max_answers_allowed` column in `domain_registry.csv` and compare with `where answer_cardinality>max_answers_allowed`.
‚ÄĘ **Split-horizon internal views** ‚ÄĒ Distinguish: queries from `corp` resolvers differ. Suppress: filter `src_ip` with `cidrmatch` to exclude internal recursion.
‚ÄĘ **Intel feed maintenance blackout** ‚ÄĒ Distinguish: vendor publishes schedule. Suppress: time-bound exception register referencing vendor ticket.
‚ÄĘ **Wildcard marketing campaign DNS** ‚ÄĒ Distinguish: CNAME chains to third-party CDNs. Suppress: lookup `nis2_dns_kfp_exceptions` keyed by campaign id for 30 days.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for Infoblox (Splunkbase 2934, DNS query and response logging)
Splunk CIM Add-on (Splunkbase 1621, Network_Resolution data model acceleration)
Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (index=dns for DNS resolution telemetry)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, Intelligence Downloads for STIX domain feeds)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

‚ÄĘ **Splunk**: Enterprise ‚Č•9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
‚ÄĘ **Indexes**: `dns`, `threat_intel` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
‚ÄĘ **Lookups**: `nis2_dns_kfp_exceptions`, `domain_registry.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_dns_kfp_exceptions','domain_registry.csv')`.
‚ÄĘ **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.28`. The control objective is: "Produces NIS2 evidence for Art.28 using NIS2 Art.28-30 domain registration, DNS integrity and information-sharing evidence." ‚ÄĒ every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC ‚ÄĒ no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `dns`, `threat_intel` under `dns:query`, `infoblox:dns`, `stix`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


‚ÄĘ **Hint 1:** Deploy Splunkbase 2934 for Infoblox or map BIND logs to `dns:query`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability ‚ÄĒ document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

‚ÄĘ **Hint 2:** Populate `domain_registry.csv`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability ‚ÄĒ document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

‚ÄĘ **Hint 3:** Inhibit alert noise with `nis2_dns_kfp_exceptions`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability ‚ÄĒ document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

‚ÄĘ **Hint 4:** Integrate STIX feeds via ES. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something ‚ÄĒ monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

‚ÄĘ **Hint 5:** Archive weekly PDF to `index=audit_evidence`. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability ‚ÄĒ document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Implement the search

For "NIS2 Art.28-30 domain registration, DNS integrity and information-sharing evidence," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself ‚ÄĒ auditors will read these rows as the official record of NIS2 control activity.

This search begins with `| inputlookup domain_registry.csv` ‚ÄĒ the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `domain_registry.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

‚ÄĘ Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
‚ÄĘ Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
‚ÄĘ Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup domain_registry.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true domain_registry_audit.csv` ‚ÄĒ this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
‚ÄĘ Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the last 24 hours) is the detection trigger ‚ÄĒ when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Network_Resolution` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Network_Resolution where nodename=Network_Resolution.DNS by Network_Resolution.query span=1h
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Network_Resolution` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC ‚ÄĒ the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.55: NIS2 Art.28-30 domain registration, DNS integrity and inform]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.55,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=dns sourcetype=dns:query earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data ‚ÄĒ it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `owner`, `status`, `evidence_id`, `clause`, `affected_asset`, `affected_service`. Run:

```spl
index=dns sourcetype=dns:query earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, owner, status, evidence_id, clause, affected_asset, affected_service
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_dns_kfp_exceptions`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_dns_kfp_exceptions
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `observed_domains`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(observed_domains) as p50 perc90(observed_domains) as p90 perc99(observed_domains) as p99 max(observed_domains) as hi
```

The current threshold `1` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 ‚ÄĒ Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.55: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 ‚ÄĒ Dns Answer Cardinality Heatmap (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 ‚ÄĒ Table Of `Query` Vs Expected Nameservers (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 ‚ÄĒ Intel Feed Single Values (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y ‚ÄĒ the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 ‚ÄĒ Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

‚ÄĘ 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours ‚ÄĒ the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

‚ÄĘ Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

‚ÄĘ Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

‚ÄĘ Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) ‚ÄĒ these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We watch the internet‚Äôs address book for your public names and compare what the world sees with what you officially expect. We also keep an eye on automated feeds of bad-domain tips so a silent stop in those feeds does not go unnoticed.
